serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of hummus as a dip is approximately 200 g (about 2–3 tablespoons per person as a starter or snack accompaniment).

nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for commercial hummus (FDC ID 172451), which is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy (USDA Foundation Foods / SR Legacy). Input values from the GrabFood record were cross-referenced against USDA SR Legacy data and found consistent within acceptable range. Input values retained as they represent a richer tahini- and olive-oil-forward preparation typical of restaurant service. USDA SR Legacy data used as the authoritative approved anchor for plausibility validation. No forbidden sources used.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Hummus (chickpea dip) GI = 6 (±4) is reported directly in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. This is a peer-reviewed journal publication produced by the University of Sydney GI research group and is an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. GL per 200 g serving = (6 × 15.2 × 2.0) / 100 = 1.8. Classified as low GI (GI < 55).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 180 mg/100 g; potassium ~228 mg/100 g; dietary fibre 4 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Hummus is suitable for individuals with hypertension; sodium at 180 mg/100 g is moderate and within DASH diet limits, while potassium from chickpeas (~228 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (4 g/100 g) actively support blood pressure reduction — choose low-sodium commercial varieties and pair with raw vegetables rather than salted crackers.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI of 6; dietary fibre 4 g/100 g; protein 6 g/100 g slows glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hummus has an exceptionally low glycemic index of 6 and a glycemic load of only 1.8 per 200 g serving, making it an excellent choice for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes — pair with non-starchy vegetables or wholemeal pita and monitor total carbohydrate intake (15.2 g/100 g) when consuming large portions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat (MUFA 4.9 g, PUFA 3.5 g per 100 g); saturated fat low at 1.6 g/100 g; zero cholesterol; dietary fibre 4 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hummus is cardioprotective: olive oil contributes heart-healthy monounsaturated fats (~4.9 g/100 g), saturated fat is low at 1.6 g/100 g, cholesterol is zero, and dietary fibre at 4 g/100 g supports LDL reduction — regular consumption as a replacement for saturated-fat-rich dips is consistent with AHA dietary guidelines for cardiovascular risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory MUFA from olive oil; polyphenols from garlic and lemon juice; low saturated fat 1.6 g/100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Hummus is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; olive oil provides oleocanthal with COX-inhibiting anti-inflammatory properties, chickpeas support gut microbiome diversity linked to immune regulation, and saturated fat is very low at 1.6 g/100 g — avoid pairing with deep-fried accompaniments that introduce pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hummus is a smooth, thick dip or spread originating from the Levant region of the Middle East, widely consumed across Turkish, Lebanese, Israeli, Syrian, and broader Mediterranean cuisines. It is prepared by blending cooked or canned chickpeas (garbanzo beans) with tahini (sesame seed paste), olive oil, fresh lemon juice, garlic, and salt until a creamy, homogeneous consistency is achieved. The texture is velvety and melt-in-the-mouth, with a rich, nutty, and mildly garlicky flavour profile balanced by the brightness of lemon juice. Hummus is typically served as a dip with pita bread, raw vegetables, or flatbreads, and is also used as a spread in wraps and sandwiches. Nutritionally, hummus is a nutrient-dense food: per 100 g it provides approximately 180 kcal, 6 g protein, 11.2 g fat (predominantly heart-healthy unsaturated fats from olive oil and tahini), 15.2 g carbohydrate, and 4 g dietary fibre. Its glycemic index is exceptionally low at 6 (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008), making it highly suitable for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium at 180 mg/100 g is moderate and compatible with DASH diet principles for hypertension management. The high fibre content and favourable fat profile support LDL cholesterol reduction relevant to cardiovascular disease prevention. Hummus is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and contains sesame as a declared allergen via tahini. It is widely available in Singapore through Middle Eastern and Turkish restaurants and supermarkets.

Can I eat hummus if I have high blood pressure?

Is hummus suitable for heart disease patients?

Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact preparation (restaurant/GrabFood) was not directly measured in an approved primary source; USDA SR Legacy data used as the closest approved anchor with adaptation documented.
